source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 14.4-14.4.1 DNF basics (pp. 261-263)

questions.q: What is DNF, and what does it succeed?
options: Dandified Yum - the successor to YUM (Yellow dog Updater Modified)
Debian Network Fetcher, successor to apt
Dynamic Node Framework, successor to rpm
Data Name Finder, successor to DNS
questions.why: DNF (Dandified Yum) is the successor of YUM and the default package manager on Red Hat-family distros.

questions.q: What is DNF's equivalent in the Debian world?
questions.why: Red Hat distros use dnf; Debian-family distros use apt.

questions.q: Install the packages wget and tree from the repository, answering yes automatically.
questions.why: dnf install pulls from the repository (resolving dependencies); -y auto-answers yes.

questions.q: Search the repositories for packages matching 'vim'.
questions.why: dnf search finds packages in the repository matching the given string.

questions.q: Upgrade all upgradable packages on the system, answering yes automatically.
questions.why: dnf update (or upgrade) upgrades packages; with no package name it upgrades everything upgradable.

questions.q: Automatically remove packages that were installed as dependencies but are no longer needed.
questions.why: dnf autoremove deletes now-unused dependency packages.

questions.q: How does dnf handle dependencies compared with rpm?
options: dnf resolves and installs dependencies automatically from the repository; rpm does not
rpm resolves them; dnf does not
questions.why: dnf interacts with repositories and resolves dependencies automatically, unlike the local-only rpm.
